Figure.title: Figure
Figure.description: Represents a key statistic, achievement, or important number to be displayed on the website.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Figure entity.

icon.description: The icon identifier (e.g., FontAwesome class name) associated with the figure.

value.description: The numerical or text value of the statistic (e.g., '95%', '3 Campus').

description.description: A brief textual description explaining what the figure represents.

Program.title: Program
Program.description: Represents an academic program or course offered by the institution, showcased in the 'Nos Programmes' section.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Program entity.

title.description: The name of the program (e.g., 'Bachelor Packaging').

imageUrl.description: The URL of an image representing the program on its card.

shortDescription.description: A concise summary or introductory description of the program.

detailPageUrl.description: The URL to the full details page for this program.

NewsArticle.title: NewsArticle
NewsArticle.description: Represents a news article or blog post for the 'Actualités' section, including key display information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the NewsArticle entity.

title.description: The main title of the news article.

publishDate.description: The date and time when the article was published.

excerpt.description: A short excerpt or summary of the article content, generated by the AI summary tool.

imageUrl.description: The URL of a featured image for the news article card.

articleUrl.description: The URL to the full content page of the article.

Partner.title: Partner
Partner.description: Represents a partner organization or company whose logo is displayed in the partners carousel.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Partner entity.

name.description: The official name of the partner organization.

logoUrl.description: The URL of the partner's logo image, suitable for display in the carousel.

websiteUrl.description: The URL to the partner's official website.

definition.description: Stores key statistics and achievements to be displayed on the website. All documents are publicly readable. Write access is restricted to administrators.
Contains academic programs and courses. All documents are publicly readable. Write access is restricted to administrators.
Holds news articles and blog posts for the 'Actualités' section. All documents are publicly readable. Write access is restricted to administrators.
Manages partner organizations and their logos for the partners carousel. All documents are publicly readable. Write access is restricted to administrators.
Identifies users with administrative privileges. The existence of a document in this collection for a given `uid` grants write access to public content collections, adhering to the DBAC principle.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who is an administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure prioritizes clarity, security, and scalability for a static website that will eventually pull dynamic data. It adheres to all core design principles and strategy mandates, especially Authorization Independence and QAPs.

**Authorization Independence:** For this specific application, where all core data (Figures, Programs, News Articles, Partners) is publicly readable and administratively writable, Authorization Independence is achieved inherently. There are no complex hierarchical authorization dependencies or subcollections requiring parent data for access checks. Each document's access is determined solely by its presence within a top-level collection, which carries a uniform security posture (public read, admin write). This eliminates the need for `get()` calls in security rules, as all authorization context is self-contained within the document or at the collection level. For administrative access, an `/admins/{userId}` collection is introduced; the existence of a document for a `request.auth.uid` in this collection grants administrative privileges, again, avoiding hierarchical lookups.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** The structure ensures secure `list` operations by implementing **Structural Segregation**. All documents within a given collection (e.g., `/programs`, `/newsArticles`) share the same security requirements: they are all intended for public consumption. This means that a `list` operation on any of these collections will only ever return publicly accessible data, thus eliminating the need for security rules to act as data filters. If there were private drafts or sensitive items, they would be placed in separate, secured collections (e.g., `/users/{uid}/drafts`), but this is not applicable to the current public-facing entities. This design ensures that all data returned by a `list` query is authorized by the rules.

**Overall Strategy:**
*   **Flat Collections:** `figures`, `programs`, `newsArticles`, and `partners` are all top-level collections. This simplifies rule writing tremendously, as each collection can have a straightforward `allow read: true;` for public access and `allow write: if request.auth.uid in get(/databases/$(database)/documents/admins/$(request.auth.uid));` for administrative actions.
*   **DBAC for Administration:** The `/admins/{userId}` collection serves as the central point for defining administrative roles, aligning with the Database-Backed Access Control (DBAC) principle. This ensures that authorization is managed directly within Firestore and leverages `request.auth.uid` for identity verification.
*   **Homogeneous Security Posture:** Each collection maintains a uniform security posture, making security rules simple, robust, and easy to debug. There's no mixing of public and private data within the same collection.
*   **Semantic Naming:** Collection and parameter names are clear and descriptive, enhancing debuggability and maintainability.
